coredata.dc:title: Porous silicon solar cells with 13.66% efficiency achieved by employing graphene-quantum-dots interfacial layer, doped-graphene electrode, and bathocuproine back-surface passivation layer
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Chan Wook
Shin, Dong Hee
Choi, Suk-Ho
coredata.dc:description: Formation of Porous Si (PSi) enhances the light absorption of Si thanks to the increased surface-to-volume ratio, useful for improving the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of Si-based solar cells. Here, we first employ graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an interfacial layer for better band alignment at the graphene (GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE)/PSi interface. The PCE exhibits a maximum of 13.66%, highest ever achieved in GR/PSi solar cells, by doping the GR TCE and passivating the Si back surface in addition to the GQDs interfacial layer. The use of the interfacial and the passivating layers results in the reduction of the carrier recombination at the GR TCE/PSi and Si/cathode interfaces, respectively. The cell also shows excellent long-term stability by maintaining 84% of its original PCE even in air for 30 days.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous Si
Solar cell
Graphene quantum dot
Graphene
Passivation
Band alignment
Interfacial layer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots interlayer
Preparation of graphene transparent conductive electrode
Fabrication of bathocuproine back-surface passivation layer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) grant funded by the Korea government ( MSIT ) (No. 2020R1A2C2004376 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of a typical GR/GQDs/PSi/n-Si/BCP solar cell. (b)-(c) J-V curves of the bare-graphene solar cell without/with GQDs under dark and AM 1.5 G photo irradiation at 100 mW cm−2, respectively. The inset in (c) shows the average efficiency for 10 solar cells without/with GQDs. (d) Energy band diagram of the cell.

(a) Photo J-V curves, (b) EQE spectra/integrated Jsc, and (c) Dark I-V curves of TFSA-GR/GQDs/PSi/Si solar cells for various ND. Regions I, II, and III in (c) illustrate how the different components: shunt resistance (Rsh), ideality factor (n), and series resistance (Rse) of the solar cell equivalent circuit dominate the I-V response at different voltages. (d) Rsh, n, and Rse parameters of the solar cells as functions of ND.

(a) Photo J-V curve of the TFSA-GR/GQDs/PSi/Si solar cell with BCP under simulated AM 1.5 illumination at 100 mW cm−2 (b) EQE spectra/integrated Jsc of the cell with BCP. (c) Photo J-V curves of the solar cells of 9, 16, and 25 mm2 active areas. The inset shows photos of the solar cells. (d) Degradation of the PCE during 30 days for different active area.
Photovoltaic parameters of bare-GR/PSi/n-Si solar cells without/with GQDs layer.
Summary of optical and electrical properties of TFSA-GR TCE with different ND = 0, 10, 20, and 30 mM.
Photovoltaic parameters of TFSA-GR/GQDs/PSi/n-Si solar cells of 9 mm2 active area for various ND.
Photovoltaic parameters of TFSA-GR/GQDs/PSi/n-Si/BCP solar cells of 9, 16, and 25 mm2 active areas for ND = 20 mM.

head.ce:title: Porous silicon solar cells with 13.66% efficiency achieved by employing graphene-quantum-dots interfacial layer, doped-graphene electrode, and bathocuproine back-surface passivation layer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Science and Technology (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Science and Technology (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Institute of Natural Sciences, and Integrated Education Institute for Frontier Materials (BK21 Four), Kyung Hee University, Yongin 17104, Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk 36729, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Andong National University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Andong National University, Andong, Gyeongbuk, 36729, Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors have contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formation of Porous Si (PSi) enhances the light absorption of Si thanks to the increased surface-to-volume ratio, useful for improving the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of Si-based solar cells. Here, we first employ graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as an interfacial layer for better band alignment at the graphene (GR) transparent conductive electrode (TCE)/PSi interface. The PCE exhibits a maximum of 13.66%, highest ever achieved in GR/PSi solar cells, by doping the GR TCE and passivating the Si back surface in addition to the GQDs interfacial layer. The use of the interfacial and the passivating layers results in the reduction of the carrier recombination at the GR TCE/PSi and Si/cathode interfaces, respectively. The cell also shows excellent long-term stability by maintaining 84% of its original PCE even in air for 30 days.

ce:section.ce:para: The solar energy market is still being dominated by crystalline-Si solar cells due to the unsurpassed properties such as abundant resources, non-toxicity, high efficiency, and matchless long-term stability [1]. Despite a sharp drop in the module prices over the last several years [2], Si solar cells are still recognized as an expensive solar energy source, mostly originating from the complex manufacturing processes. In addition, Si solar cells are limited in their further improvement for enhancing the photovoltaic performance due to the inherent light absorption properties of Si [3,4]. As another approach, formation of nanostructured Si such as porous Si (PSi) is useful for improving the light absorption of Si thanks to the increased large surface-to-volume ratio, thereby possibly enhancing the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of Si-based solar cells [5–10]. Graphene (GR) is widely used as a transparent conductive electrode (TCE) in optoelectronic devices [11,12] due to its excellent properties such as high optical transmittance (T), tunable work function, good flexibility/stretchability, and chemical/mechanical stability [13], However, high sheet resistance (Rs) of GR limits the device performance [14]. Chemical doping is regarded as an effective method to improve the Rs of GR. Despite many studies on the use of doped-GR TCE in PSi [15–17] and bulk-Si [17] solar cells, their PCE is still relatively small due to the big difference in the work function between GR TCE and Si-related material. Recently, various kinds of interfacial layers were employed to match the band profile at the GR TCE/Si interface, effective for increasing the PCE [6,18,19].

Unlike semi-metallic GR with no band gap, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a semiconductor with a band gap, based on quantum confinement and edge effects [20,21]. An interfacial layer with a band gap like GQDs, suitable for the band alignment between GR TCE and Si, can be selected to reduce the electron-hole recombination at the interface, resulting in the enhancement of the PCE [22]. Furthermore, the lifetime of the hot carriers in GQDs is relatively long, useful for efficient hot carrier harvesting and multiple carrier generation [23,24]. Another technique for improving the PCE of Si-based solar cells is the back passivation that can block the carrier recombination at the Si back surface [25–27]. Bathocuproine (BCP) is well known as a material for blocking holes as well as for smoothing the movement of electrons at the back surface in organic solar cells [28,29]. Similar effects are expected to be brought about at the Si/BCP interface of Si-based solar cells. Here, we first report a structure of (trifluoromethanesulfonyl)-amide-doped GR (TFSA-GR) TCE/GQDs/PSi/n-Si/BCP for solar cell application. The cell exhibits maximum PCE of 13.66% under 100 mW cm−2 irradiation, resulting from the reduced carrier recombination by employing GQDs and BCP layers at the GR/PSi and Si/cathode interfaces, respectively. The cell also shows good long-term stability by maintaining 84% of its original PCE even in air for 30 days.

High-porosity PSi was fabricated by conventional metal assisted chemical etching (MacEtch) [30]. Au nanoparticles (NPs) were first deposited on a cleaned n-Si wafer (phosphorus doped Si, 1–5 ohm-cm) using DC metal sputtering for 5 s. The NPs/n-Si was then immersed in a pre-prepared etchant (HF: H2O2 = 1: 1) for only 5 s. During this process, the Si parts beneath the Ag NPs were selectively etched down to form PSi on the surface of n-Si. Here, the Au NPs played as a role of a catalyst for the MacEtch. The remnant Au NPs on the surface of the PSi was completely removed in aqua regia solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of graphene quantum dots interlayer
ce:section.ce:para: To form a layer of GQDs on the PSi surface, 200-mL GQDs solution was dropped/spin-coated onto the PSi surface at 1500 rpm for 40 s, which was then annealed at 100 °C for 1 min. Here, the GQDs of 15 nm average diameter was purchased from Dotz Nano Ltd. By repeating this procedure up to 12 times, it was possible to form a film of GQDs without pinholes on the PSi surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene transparent conductive electrode
ce:section.ce:para: GR layers were grown on 70 μm-thick Cu foils (Wacopa, 99.8 purity) in a graphite-heater-based chemical-vapor-deposition quartz tube furnace at a growth temperature of 1000 °C under mixed-gas (Ar:H2:CH4 = 325:10:20 sccm) flow at a total pressure of 3 Torr. To prepare a poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) sacrificial film, PMMA powder (0.46 g) was first added to a chlorobenzene solution (10 mL) in a beaker, which was then spun at 1000 rpm for 10 h for the powder to be well dissolved. The solution was dropped/spin-coated on a GR/Cu foil at 4500 rpm for 1 min, which was then heat-treated at 180 °C for 2 h to form a PMMA film. To separate the GR sheet from the substrate, the PMMA/GR/Cu stack was immersed in a 1 M FeCl3 solution for 3 h, and the remaining PMMA/GR was then sufficiently washed in deionized (DI) water at 80 °C. The PMMA/GR was transferred to the GQDs/PSi/n-Si substrate and heat-treated at 120 °C for 30 min for the perfect contact. Finally, the PMMA was removed by acetone. TFSA powder, p-type dopant for GR, was dissolved in nitromethane at room temperature to prepare the TFSA solution. The doping concentration (ND) was controlled by adjusting the amount of the powder. The TFSA solution was dropped/spin-coated on the entire GR surface at 2500 rpm for 1 min, which was then heat-treated at 100 °C for 1 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of bathocuproine back-surface passivation layer
ce:section.ce:para: To prevent electron-hole recombination between Si wafer and the cathode, BCP powder/ethanol solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was dropped/spin-coated on the back side of Si wafer at 4000 rpm for 40 s to form the BCP layer. InGa paste (Gallium-indium eutectic, Sigma Aldrich) was then spread on the BCP layer as a cathode.

Plan- and cross-sectional-view images of PSi were obtained by using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss, model LEO SUPRA 55). Raman-scattering behaviors of PSi and GR were analyzed in a Raman spectroscopy system with a 532 nm laser. The T, Rs, and work function of GR were measured by ultraviolet (UV)-visible near-infrared (NIR) optical spectroscopy (Agilent Varian, model cary 5000), 4-probe van der Pauw method (Dasol eng, model FPP-HS8-40K), and Kelvin probe force microscopy (Park System, model XE-100), respectively. The size/distribution and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) spectrum of GQDs were obtained through a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with EELS mapping function. The absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs were measured by UV–visible NIR optical spectrometer and PL spectroscopy system with a 325 nm laser, respectively. The thickness of the GQDs layer was evaluated through non-contact-mode atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park System, model XE-100). The electrical characterizations of the cells were done by a Keithley 2400 source meter. Photovoltaic parameters of the cells were extracted from a solar simulator (McScinece, model K201) with stable 100 mW cm−2 illumination. External quantum efficiency (EQE) of the cells was measured by a Xenon arc lamp (Oriel Apex Illuminator, Newport) and a monochromator (Cornerstone 260, Newport).

Fig. 1(a) shows a schematic diagram of a typical GR TCE/GQDs/PSi/n-Si/BCP/InGa solar cell. In the Raman spectrum of pristine GR, the relative (2D/G) band intensity was estimated to be ~2.33 and the D band was very weak, indicating good-quality monolayer GR (Supporting Information, Fig. S1a) [31]. The T of pristine GR was 97.7% at a wavelength of 550 nm, meaning excellent transparency (Supporting Information, Fig. S1b). High-magnification TEM images demonstrated the existence of GQDs of about 5 nm in size, defined as 1/2 x (width +length) of the GQD (Supporting Information, Fig. S2a-b). The peaks corresponding to π* (~285 eV) and σ* (~291 eV) states of the GQDs were clearly observed in the K-edge EELS spectrum of GQDs [32,33] (Supporting Information, Fig. S2c). In the GQDs, light was absorbed in UV region (365 nm) and was emitted in the visible region (450 nm), a phenomenon known as a down-conversion process, commonly observed in GQDs (Supporting Information, Fig. S2d). In the previous literature [32,33], we proposed an approach for optimally coating GQDs as a film without pinholes on substrates. The thickness of the GQDs layer was measured to be about ~60 nm by analyzing the AFM image (Supporting Information, Fig. S2e). By plan- and cross-sectional-view SEM images, the surface of the PSi was shown to be highly porous (Supporting Information, Fig. S3a), and its depth was estimated to be approximately ~60 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S3b). Here, the preparation conditions for optimizing the porosity and thickness of the PSi were controlled for the highest PCE. Raman spectrum of the PSi (peaked at 513 cm−1) was 7 cm−1 blue-shifted and widened compared to that of bulk Si (peaked at 520 cm−1), consistent with previously-reported results (Supporting Information, Fig. S3c) [10].

As a first step, the effect of GQDs on the performance of a typical pristine GR/PSi/Si solar cell structure was studied by locating the GQDs between the GR and PSi. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the dark current is remarkably reduced by including the GQDs in the cell, indicating smaller saturation current density (J0), useful for increasing the PCE due to the enhanced blocking of the carrier recombination. Fig. 1(c) shows current density-voltage (J-V) behaviors of the cells with/without GQDs under 100 mW cm−2 illumination. The cell with GQDs exhibits much better photovoltaic characteristics of 0.494 V open-circuit voltage (Voc), 28.01 mA/cm2 short circuit current density (Jsc), and 59.50% fill factor (FF), and 8.23% PCE, as shown in Table 1. The inset in Fig. 1(c) shows statistical deviations for the average PCEs of 4.37 ± 0.84 and 7.76 ± 0.47%, calculated from the J-V curves for 10 devices without/with GQDs, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S4).

To understand the role of the GQDs in the device performance, the energy band diagram of the bare GR/GQDs/PSi/Si solar cell is described in Fig. 1(d). Here, the work function of the GR was measured by us, and other band parameters were referred from previous literature [6–10,22]. When light is irradiated, the electron-hole pairs are generated mostly in PSi and separated into electrons and holes at the GQDs/PSi interface by the built-in electric field. The holes are transferred through the GQDs layer and then are collected by the GR TCE whilst the electrons are collected by the bottom InGa electrode. As shown in the band diagram, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital level of the GQDs is significantly higher than the Fermi level of the PSi, thereby effectively preventing the electrons from moving to the GR TCE, resulting in the reduction of the recombination at the GR TCE interface. On the other hand, the transport of the holes seems to be blocked from the GQDs layer to the PSi due to the unfavorable barrier at the GQDs/PSi interface. However, the hole transport is available through the GQDs layer in the other way, as explained below. It is well known that oxygen-related molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the GQDs [22], forming defect energy levels close to the highest occupied molecular orbital (the dotted line in the band gap of the GQDs). Therefore, the holes can be collected effectively to the GR TCE by tunneling through the defect levels of the GQDs. This explains why the PCE is enhanced by including the GQDs in the cell. These results further suggest that the GQDs layer cannot act only as a hole transport layer but also as an electron blocking layer to reduce the carrier recombination in the device.

As a second step, the GR TCE was doped with TFSA by varying ND to improve the PCE. TFSA dopant accepts an electron charge from the GR, thereby increasing its work function, resulting in p-type doping of the GR [8,9]. Compared to other types of doped GR, TFSA-GR TCE showed better characteristics as a TCE, especially in long-term/flexible stabilities [6,8]. The ND-dependent variations of T and Rs for the TFSA-GR are summarized in Table 2. The Rs and T for various ND were used to evaluate the performance of the TFSA-GR as a TCE based on the following equation: T = {1 + (Zo/2Rs)(σOP /σDC)}−2 , where Z0 is impedance of free space, σOP is optical conductivity, and σDC is direct conductivity [34]. The σDC/σOP value was higher than the minimum industry standard value (~35) for all ND, and reached its maximum of 61 at ND = 20 mM.

Fig. 2(a) shows photo J–V curves of the solar cell for various ND. The extracted photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 3. The cell exhibits best properties of 0.529 Voc, 32.03 mA·cm−2 Jsc, 72.89% FF, and 12.35% PCE at ND = 20 mM. The ND-dependent behaviors of the PCE are consistent with those of the σDC/σOP, as shown in Table 2. Fig. 2(b) shows ND-dependent EQE spectra and integrated Jsc. The integrated Jsc is well correlated with the measured one, as shown in Table 3. By employing the TFSA-GR TCE, the EQE is significantly improved over a broad wavelength range from 300 to 1100 nm. With increasing ND, the sheet resistance is sharply reduced with the T being slightly changed, as shown in Table 2. This enhances the charge separation/collection efficiencies, resulting in the increase of the EQE.

In order to know why best PCE is achieved at ND = 20 mM, shunt resistance (Rsh), ideality factor (n), and series resistance (Rse) were extracted from the dark I-V curves, as shown in Fig. 2(c). At low voltage (region I, V < 0.1 V), intermediate voltage (region II, V = 0.1~0.6 V), and high voltage (region III, V > 0.6 V), the I-V curve is usually determined by Rsh, n, and Rse [35]. At ND = 20 mM. the Rsh is maximized to 5521 ohm cm2 whilst the Rse is minimized to 2.39 ohm cm2, as shown in Fig. 2(d). As ND increases, the electrical conductivity of the TFSA-GR is enhanced due to the reduction of the ohmic losses whist the T is reduced and the Fermi level is lowered. Based on this ND-dependent trade-off correlation, larger Rsh and smaller Rse result in better FF. Higher Rsh induces smaller recombination at the interfaces [35] and the reduction of the Rse facilitates the charge collection [36], thereby leading to the enhancement of the photovoltaic parameters including the FF. The n was evaluated from the thermionic emission model-based formula: J = J0 [exp (eV/nkBT) - 1], where J0 is reverse-bias saturation current density, e is elementary charge, kB is Boltzmann’s constant, and T is temperature. The n is calculated to be 2.86, 2.31, 2.01, and 2.20 for ND = 0, 10, 20, and 30 mM, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(d). Due to the trade-off correlation between the ND-dependent Rs, T, and energy barrier at the TFSA-GR/SQDs interface which depends on the work function of the TFSA-GR, as shown in Table 2, the n is smallest at nD = 20 mM, consistent with our previous report [37]. In solar cells, smaller n means less interface recombination, resulting in higher PCE. These dark I-V characteristics are also consistent with the ND-dependent behaviors of the σDC/σOP, as shown in Table 2.

As a third step, it is important to insert an interfacial layer not only between TFSA-GR and PSi but also between Si and InGa cathode to increase the PCE. Fig. 3(a) shows photo J-V curve of the cell with BCP layer between Si and InGa cathode, resulting in the enhancement of the PCE by about 10% from 12.35% to 13.66% for the cell of 9 mm2 active area, as shown in Table 4. Fig. 3(b) exhibits the EQE of the cell with BCP. The Jsc is calculated to be 30.90 mA/cm2 from the EQE spectra. As shown in the energy band diagram (Fig. 1(d)), the electrons can move better to the cathode by turning back at the GQDs/PSi interface whilst the holes are blocked by the BCP layer. This explains why the PCE is enhanced by the BCP layer.

As a final step, the PCE was measured depending on the size of the active area of the cell. The active area was adjusted according to the device size and mask size. Fig. 3(c) shows the effect of the active area (9, 16, and 25 mm2) on the J-V characteristics. All the photovoltaic parameters decrease as the active area increases. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that the PCE is reduced only by about 10.5% (absolutely from 13.66% to 12.37%) as the active area decreases about 3 times, as summarized in Table 4. As the active area increases, the Jsc and FF decrease due to the smaller charge collection [36] and larger charge recombination [35], respectively. This explains why the PCE decreases at larger active area. To check the long-term stability of the cell, the J-V curves of the TFSA-GR/GQDs/PSi/n-Si/BCP cells was monitored for 30 days (Fig. S5†). The solar cell maintains ~85% of its initial PCE regardless of the size of the active layer, as summarized in Fig. 3(d) (Supporting Information, Table S1). This excellent stability possibly originates from that of the TFSA-GR TCE [37] as well as from its effective role as a protecting layer for the GQDs in atmosphere. These results suggest that the combination of TFSA-GR, GQDs, and BCP is very useful for enhancing the PCE and durability of PSi solar cells, compared to previously-reported GR/PSi-based cells (Supporting Information, Table S2) [6–10].

TFSA-GR TCE/GQDs/PSi/Si/BCP/InGa solar cells were first successfully fabricated by employing GQDs and BCP as interfacial and passivating layers, resulting in the reduction of the carrier recombination at the TFSA-GR TCE/PSi and Si/InGa interfaces, respectively. The transparent conductive properties of the TFSA-GR were optimized by controlling ND, resulting in maximum PCE of 13.66% at ND = 20 mM, highest ever achieved in GR/PSi solar cells. The PCE was reduced only by about 10.5% from 13.66% to 12.37% as the active area decreased about 3 times from 9 to 25 mm2. The cell lost only 16% of its original PCE even in air for 30 days, indicating excellent long-term stability.

C.W. Jang and D.H. Shin performed device design, device fabrication, and characterization for the solar cells. S.-H.C. initiated, supervised the work, and wrote the paper. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2020R1A2C2004376).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.160311.
